summary: This post explains why UML uses the term 'multiplicity' instead of 'cardinality' when describing relationships between entities. While data modelers commonly use 'cardinality' to describe how many entities can be linked (like one-to-many), the UML designers chose 'multiplicity' because it's more technically accurate according to dictionary definitions. Cardinality properly refers to the actual number of elements in a set, whereas multiplicity describes the allowable range of values that cardinality can take. The distinction matters because UML aims for precise terminology in its formal specification.
key_points: Data modeling traditionally uses 'cardinality' to describe relationship constraints (one-to-many, etc.)
UML deliberately chose 'multiplicity' over the more common 'cardinality' term
The dictionary definition of cardinality refers to actual count, not allowable ranges
Multiplicity is formally defined as a lower and upper bound specification
money_quotes: UML avoids the term cardinality preferring to use multiplicity.
The reason for the change is that the dictionary definition of cardinality is 'the number of elements in a particular set or other grouping'.
According to this the data modeling usage is actually wrong.
Rumbaugh defines multiplicity as 'A specification of the range of allowable cardinality values - the size - that a set may assume'.
key_insight: The data modeling community's use of 'cardinality' is technically incorrect—cardinality means actual count, while multiplicity correctly describes the allowable range of counts in a relationship.
